title: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen

items.title: Why most lymphadenopathy in primary care needs few tests and no biopsy
items.content:
Lymphadenopathy is common in primary care, and the overwhelming majority of cases are benign.
In primary care practice, more than two-thirds of patients have nonspecific causes or upper respiratory illnesses, and fewer than 1% have a malignancy.
In a referral study, 84% had a benign diagnosis and only 16% a malignancy (lymphoma or metastatic adenocarcinoma); of the benign cases, 63% were nonspecific/reactive.
This low pretest probability is why the vast majority of patients require few diagnostic tests, and in primary care fewer than 5% will need a biopsy.
keyPoints: >2/3 of primary-care lymphadenopathy is nonspecific or URI-related; <1% is malignant
In referral practice 84% benign, 16% malignant (lymphoma or metastatic adenocarcinoma)
<5% of primary-care patients need a biopsy; at least half need no labs
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (Lymphadenopathy): "In primary care practice, more than two-thirds of patients with lymphadenopathy have nonspecific causes or upper respiratory illnesses (viral or bacterial) and <1% have a malignancy."

items.title: How to recognize lymph nodes that are normal and need no workup
items.content:
Some palpable nodes are normal variants that do not warrant evaluation.
Soft, flat, submandibular nodes (<1 cm) are often palpable in healthy children and young adults.
Healthy adults may have palpable inguinal nodes of up to 2 cm, which are considered normal.
Nodes <1.0 cm² in area (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm or less) are almost always secondary to benign, nonspecific reactive causes and should simply be observed after excluding infectious mononucleosis and/or toxoplasmosis, unless there are signs of systemic illness.
keyPoints: Submandibular nodes <1 cm (children/young adults) and inguinal nodes up to 2 cm (adults) are normal
Nodes ≤1.0 cm² are almost always benign reactive
Observe small nodes after excluding mononucleosis/toxoplasmosis unless systemic features present
items.reference: LYMPHADENOPATHY: "Soft, flat, submandibular nodes (<1 cm) are often palpable in healthy children and young adults; healthy adults may have palpable inguinal nodes of up to 2 cm, which are considered normal."

items.title: Why patient age shifts the differential between benign and malignant causes
items.content:
Age is one of the most powerful discriminators in lymphadenopathy.
Children and young adults usually have benign disorders — viral or bacterial upper respiratory infections, infectious mononucleosis, toxoplasmosis, and in some countries tuberculosis.
After age 50, the incidence of malignant disorders increases and that of benign disorders decreases.
This is why age >40 carries positive predictive value, and age <40 negative predictive value, in models that decide who needs biopsy.
keyPoints: Young patients: mostly benign (URI, mononucleosis, toxoplasmosis, TB)
After age 50 malignant causes rise and benign causes fall
Age >40 has positive, age <40 negative, predictive value for needing biopsy
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "In contrast, after age 50, the incidence of malignant disorders increases and that of benign disorders decreases."

items.title: Drug hypersensitivity as a reversible cause of lymphadenopathy
items.content:
Medications are an important and reversible cause of lymphadenopathy that is easy to overlook.
The history should specifically include use of drugs such as diphenylhydantoin (phenytoin).
Drug hypersensitivity causes of lymphadenopathy include diphenylhydantoin, hydralazine, allopurinol, primidone, gold, and carbamazepine, among others.
Identifying a culprit drug can spare the patient an unnecessary biopsy.
keyPoints: Always take a drug history (e.g., phenytoin) in lymphadenopathy
Culprits include phenytoin, hydralazine, allopurinol, primidone, gold, carbamazepine
Drug-induced lymphadenopathy is reversible — recognizing it avoids biopsy
items.reference: TABLE 70-1 Diseases Associated with Lymphadenopathy: "Drug hypersensitivity—diphenylhydantoin, hydralazine, allopurinol, primidone, gold, carbamazepine, etc."

items.title: Why the localized-versus-generalized distinction has limited diagnostic value
items.content:
Localized (regional) adenopathy involves a single anatomic area; generalized adenopathy is involvement of three or more noncontiguous lymph node areas.
Many causes can produce either pattern, so this distinction is of limited utility in the differential diagnosis.
Nonetheless, generalized lymphadenopathy is frequently associated with nonmalignant disorders — infectious mononucleosis (EBV or CMV), toxoplasmosis, AIDS, other viral infections, SLE, and mixed connective tissue disease — and also with acute and chronic lymphocytic leukemias and malignant lymphomas in adults.
keyPoints: Generalized = 3 or more noncontiguous node areas
Either pattern can arise from many causes, limiting discriminatory value
Generalized adenopathy: mono, toxoplasmosis, AIDS, SLE, MCTD — and leukemias/lymphomas
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Generalized adenopathy has been defined as involvement of three or more noncontiguous lymph node areas."

items.title: Why supraclavicular and scalene adenopathy is always abnormal
items.content:
The site of regional adenopathy gives strong etiologic clues, and some sites are red flags.
Enlargement of supraclavicular and scalene nodes is always abnormal.
Because these nodes drain the lung and retroperitoneal space, they can reflect lymphomas, other cancers, or infectious processes arising in those areas.
Virchow's node is an enlarged left supraclavicular node infiltrated with metastatic cancer from a gastrointestinal primary; supraclavicular metastases also arise from lung, breast, testis, or ovarian cancers.
keyPoints: Supraclavicular/scalene node enlargement is always abnormal — investigate
They drain lung and retroperitoneum → lymphoma, cancer, or infection
Virchow's node = left supraclavicular node with metastatic GI cancer
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Enlargement of supraclavicular and scalene nodes is always abnormal."

items.title: How node location localizes the underlying process
items.content:
Specific drainage patterns point to specific causes.
Occipital adenopathy often reflects a scalp infection; preauricular adenopathy accompanies conjunctival infections and cat-scratch disease.
The neck is the most frequent site of regional adenopathy and most causes are benign (URIs, oral/dental lesions, mononucleosis), with malignant causes being metastatic head and neck, breast, lung, and thyroid primaries.
Axillary adenopathy is usually from injuries or infections of the ipsilateral arm (malignant causes: melanoma, lymphoma, and breast cancer in women); inguinal adenopathy usually follows lower-extremity infection/trauma or STDs.
keyPoints: Occipital → scalp; preauricular → conjunctival infection / cat-scratch
Cervical: mostly benign; malignant = metastatic head/neck, breast, lung, thyroid
Axillary: arm injury/infection, melanoma, lymphoma, breast cancer; inguinal: leg infection/trauma, STDs
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Axillary adenopathy is usually due to injuries or localized infections of the ipsilateral upper extremity. Malignant causes include melanoma or lymphoma and, in women, breast cancer."

items.title: How node size predicts malignant or granulomatous disease
items.content:
Size is a measurable discriminator for which nodes need biopsy.
In younger patients (9–25 years), a maximum diameter >2 cm predicted that biopsy would reveal malignant or granulomatous disease.
Another study found a node size of 2.25 cm² (1.5 cm × 1.5 cm) the best size limit for distinguishing malignant or granulomatous lymphadenopathy from other causes.
In contrast, nodes ≤1.0 cm² should generally be observed.
keyPoints: >2 cm diameter in young patients predicts malignant/granulomatous biopsy yield
2.25 cm² (1.5×1.5 cm) is a useful upper size cutoff for concern
Nodes ≤1.0 cm² are observed (after excluding mono/toxoplasmosis)
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "a maximum diameter of >2 cm served as one discriminant for predicting that the biopsy would reveal malignant or granulomatous disease."

items.title: How node texture distinguishes lymphoma, metastatic cancer, and inflammation
items.content:
The feel of a node narrows the differential.
Tenderness occurs when the capsule is stretched during rapid enlargement, usually secondary to an inflammatory process (though acute leukemia can also cause rapid, painful enlargement).
Nodes involved by lymphoma tend to be large, discrete, symmetric, rubbery, firm, mobile, and nontender.
Nodes containing metastatic cancer are often hard, nontender, and nonmovable because of fixation to surrounding tissues.
keyPoints: Tender nodes: capsular stretch from rapid (usually inflammatory) enlargement
Lymphoma nodes: rubbery, firm, mobile, nontender
Metastatic nodes: hard, fixed, nontender
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Nodes involved by lymphoma tend to be large, discrete, symmetric, rubbery, firm, mobile, and nontender. Nodes containing metastatic cancer are often hard, nontender, and nonmovable because of fixation to surrounding tissues."

items.title: Why enlarged intra-abdominal/retroperitoneal nodes warrant concern
items.content:
Deep adenopathy carries a different prior probability than superficial nodes.
Enlarged intraabdominal or retroperitoneal nodes are usually malignant.
Although tuberculosis may present as mesenteric lymphadenitis, these masses usually contain lymphomas or, in young men, germ cell tumors.
Nonsuperficial (thoracic or abdominal) adenopathy is usually detected as the result of a symptom-directed diagnostic workup.
keyPoints: Intra-abdominal/retroperitoneal nodes are usually malignant
Most contain lymphoma, or germ cell tumors in young men (TB can mimic as mesenteric lymphadenitis)
Deep adenopathy is usually found via symptom-directed workup
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Enlarged intraabdominal or retroperitoneal nodes are usually malignant."

items.title: How ultrasonographic L/S ratio discriminates benign from malignant cervical nodes
items.content:
Imaging helps characterize cervical nodes in head and neck cancer.
CT and MRI are comparably accurate (65–90%) in diagnosing metastases to cervical lymph nodes, and MRI offers no clear advantage over CT.
Ultrasonography measures the long (L) and short (S) axes: an L/S ratio of <2.0 has a sensitivity and specificity of 95% for distinguishing benign and malignant nodes in head and neck cancer.
This ratio outperforms palpation or either axis measured alone.
keyPoints: CT and MRI: 65–90% accurate for cervical nodal metastases
Ultrasound L/S ratio <2.0: 95% sensitivity and specificity in head and neck cancer
L/S ratio beats palpation or single-axis measurement
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "An L/S ratio of <2.0 has a sensitivity and a specificity of 95% for distinguishing benign and malignant nodes in patients with head and neck cancer."

items.title: Why excisional biopsy — not fine-needle aspiration — is the first diagnostic procedure
items.content:
When biopsy is indicated, technique matters for getting a diagnosis.
If a primary head and neck cancer is suspected, perform a careful ENT exam and biopsy any suspicious mucosal lesion first; if none is found, perform an excisional biopsy of the largest node.
Fine-needle aspiration should not be performed as the first diagnostic procedure because most diagnoses require more tissue than aspiration provides, and it often delays definitive diagnosis.
FNA is reserved for thyroid nodules and for confirming relapse when the primary diagnosis is already known.
keyPoints: Excisional biopsy of the largest node is the first-line procedure
FNA is NOT first-line — too little tissue, delays diagnosis
FNA reserved for thyroid nodules and confirming known-diagnosis relapse
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Fine-needle aspiration should not be performed as the first diagnostic procedure. Most diagnoses require more tissue than such aspiration can provide, and it often delays a definitive diagnosis."

items.title: Why glucocorticoids and antibiotics should not be used empirically for lymphadenopathy
items.content:
Two common reflex treatments can do harm in undiagnosed lymphadenopathy.
Glucocorticoids should not be used to treat lymphadenopathy because their lympholytic effect obscures some diagnoses (lymphoma, leukemia, Castleman's disease) and they contribute to delayed healing or activation of underlying infections.
The exception is life-threatening pharyngeal obstruction by enlarged lymphoid tissue in Waldeyer's ring occasionally seen in infectious mononucleosis.
Antibiotics are not indicated unless strong evidence of a bacterial infection is present.
keyPoints: Avoid empiric glucocorticoids — they mask lymphoma/leukemia/Castleman's and activate infection
Exception: airway-threatening Waldeyer's ring obstruction in mononucleosis
Antibiotics only with strong evidence of bacterial infection
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Glucocorticoids should not be used to treat lymphadenopathy because their lympholytic effect obscures some diagnoses (lymphoma, leukemia, Castleman's disease) and they contribute to delayed healing or activation of underlying infections."

items.title: How to safely follow benign-appearing lymphadenopathy
items.content:
Watchful waiting is appropriate when the picture points to a benign cause.
If the history and physical findings point to a benign cause, careful follow-up at a 2- to 4-week interval can be employed.
The patient should be instructed to return for reevaluation if there is an increase in the size of the nodes.
The decision to biopsy may be made early or delayed for up to two weeks; most patients do not require a biopsy and at least half require no laboratory studies.
keyPoints: Benign-appearing nodes: re-evaluate at 2–4 weeks
Patient returns sooner if nodes enlarge
Biopsy can be delayed up to ~2 weeks; most need neither biopsy nor labs
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "If the patient's history and physical findings point to a benign cause for lymphadenopathy, careful follow-up at a 2- to 4-week interval can be employed."

items.title: How the splenic cords cull senescent and damaged red cells
items.content:
The spleen's red-pulp architecture performs quality control on red cells.
Blood entering the cords must squeeze through slits in the cord lining to reach the sinuses and return to the circulation; old and damaged erythrocytes are less deformable and are retained in the cords, where they are destroyed and their components recycled.
Red cell inclusions — parasites, nuclear residua (Howell-Jolly bodies), or denatured hemoglobin (Heinz bodies) — are pinched off as cells pass through the slits, a process called pitting.
Culling and pitting occur without significant delay, as splenic transit time is only slightly slower than other organs.
keyPoints: Cords filter red cells through narrow slits; rigid old/damaged cells are retained and destroyed
Pitting removes inclusions (parasites, Howell-Jolly bodies, Heinz bodies)
Culling and pitting occur with minimal transit delay
items.reference: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN: "Old and damaged erythrocytes are less deformable and are retained in the cords, where they are destroyed and their components recycled."

items.title: Why the normal human spleen does not store or autotransfuse red cells (but holds platelets)
items.content:
Human splenic physiology differs from some animal models, which matters for interpreting cytopenias.
Unlike some animals, the normal human spleen does not sequester or store red blood cells and does not contract in response to sympathetic stimuli.
However, the normal human spleen contains approximately one-third of the total body platelets and a significant number of marginated neutrophils.
These sequestered platelets and neutrophils are available when needed to respond to bleeding or infection — and their pooling expands when the spleen enlarges, contributing to hypersplenic cytopenias.
keyPoints: Human spleen does NOT store/autotransfuse red cells or contract to adrenergic stimuli
It normally holds ~1/3 of body platelets plus marginated neutrophils
Splenic pooling of platelets/neutrophils underlies hypersplenic cytopenias when enlarged
items.reference: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN: "However, the normal human spleen does not sequester or store red blood cells and does not contract in response to sympathetic stimuli. The normal human spleen contains approximately one-third of the total body platelets and a significant number of marginated neutrophils."

items.title: Why a palpable spleen does not always mean disease
items.content:
A palpable spleen is the major sign of splenic enlargement but is not synonymous with pathology.
A palpable spleen was found in 3% of 2200 asymptomatic male freshman college students; at 3-year follow-up 30% still had a palpable spleen without increased disease prevalence, and 10-year follow-up found no lymphoid malignancies.
In some tropical countries (e.g., New Guinea) splenomegaly incidence may reach 60%.
Even when disease is present, splenomegaly may reflect a reaction rather than the primary disease — e.g., in Hodgkin's lymphoma only two-thirds of palpable spleens are actually involved by cancer.
keyPoints: 3% of healthy young men have a palpable spleen, often persistent and benign
Tropical splenomegaly incidence can reach 60%
In Hodgkin's lymphoma only ~2/3 of palpable spleens are involved by tumor
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (Splenomegaly): "a palpable spleen was found in 3% of 2200 asymptomatic, male, freshman college students. Follow-up at 3 years revealed that 30% of those students still had a palpable spleen without any increase in disease prevalence."

items.title: How imprecise palpation and percussion are for detecting splenomegaly
items.content:
Physical examination of the spleen has limited and operator-dependent accuracy.
Compared with ultrasonography or scintigraphy, sensitivity is 56–71% for palpation and 59–82% for percussion, and reproducibility among examiners is better for palpation than percussion.
Both techniques are less reliable in obese patients or those who have just eaten.
A suggested approach is to percuss first and, if positive, proceed to palpation — if the spleen is then palpable, splenomegaly is reasonably confirmed — but not all left-upper-quadrant masses are spleens (gastric/colon tumors, pancreatic/renal cysts or tumors can mimic).
keyPoints: Palpation sensitivity 56–71%; percussion 59–82% — both imprecise
Less reliable in obese or recently fed patients
Percuss first, then palpate; remember LUQ mimics (gastric/colon/pancreatic/renal)
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (Splenomegaly): "Studies comparing methods of percussion and palpation with a standard of ultrasonography or scintigraphy have revealed sensitivity of 56–71% for palpation and 59–82% for percussion."

items.title: Why ultrasonography is the procedure of choice for assessing spleen size
items.content:
When imaging is needed, ultrasound balances accuracy and practicality.
Ultrasonography is the current procedure of choice for routine assessment of spleen size (normal = maximum cephalocaudad diameter of 13 cm) because it has high sensitivity and specificity and is safe, noninvasive, quick, mobile, and less costly, and can be performed at the bedside.
Nuclear medicine scans are accurate but costly and immobile, with the unique advantage of demonstrating accessory splenic tissue.
CT and MRI accurately size the spleen and better show mass lesions, infarcts, and cysts (MRI offers no advantage over CT), but none of these techniques reliably detects patchy infiltration such as Hodgkin's lymphoma.
keyPoints: Ultrasound is first choice: accurate, safe, mobile, bedside-capable (normal ≤13 cm)
Nuclear scans uniquely show accessory spleens; CT/MRI better for focal lesions
No imaging reliably detects patchy infiltration (e.g., Hodgkin's lymphoma)
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (Splenomegaly): "The latter technique is the current procedure of choice for routine assessment of spleen size (normal = a maximum cephalocaudad diameter of 13 cm) because it has high sensitivity and specificity and is safe, noninvasive, quick, mobile, and less costly."

items.title: How splenomegaly is grouped by mechanism into hyperplasia, infiltration, and congestion
items.content:
Splenomegaly causes are organized by the mechanism of enlargement.
Hyperplasia or hypertrophy related to a splenic function: reticuloendothelial 'work hypertrophy' (hereditary spherocytosis, thalassemia) or immune hyperplasia (infectious mononucleosis, subacute bacterial endocarditis, immune thrombocytopenia, SLE, Felty's syndrome).
Infiltrative diseases: lymphomas, metastatic cancer, amyloidosis, Gaucher's disease, and myeloproliferative disorders with extramedullary hematopoiesis.
Passive congestion from decreased outflow in portal hypertension: cirrhosis, Budd-Chiari syndrome, congestive heart failure.
keyPoints: Work/immune hyperplasia: spherocytosis, thalassemia, mono, SBE, ITP, SLE, Felty's
Infiltration: lymphoma, metastasis, amyloid, Gaucher's, myeloproliferative disease
Congestion: cirrhosis, Budd-Chiari, CHF (portal hypertension)
items.reference: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: "Passive congestion due to decreased blood flow from the spleen in conditions that produce portal hypertension (cirrhosis, Budd-Chiari syndrome, congestive heart failure)."

items.question: A 28-year-old presents to primary care with a 1.2-cm mobile, mildly tender cervical node after a sore throat. Statistically, what is the most likely category of cause?
options: A nonspecific or upper respiratory (viral/bacterial) cause
items.explanation: In primary care, more than two-thirds of lymphadenopathy is nonspecific or due to upper respiratory illness, and fewer than 1% is malignant. A young patient with a small tender node after a sore throat fits the common benign pattern.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (Lymphadenopathy): "In primary care practice, more than two-thirds of patients with lymphadenopathy have nonspecific causes or upper respiratory illnesses (viral or bacterial) and <1% have a malignancy."

items.question: Which lymph node finding should always be regarded as abnormal and prompt investigation?
options: A 1.5-cm soft inguinal node in a healthy adult
A 0.8-cm submandibular node in a young adult
A 0.9-cm mobile tender cervical node after pharyngitis
items.explanation: Enlargement of supraclavicular (and scalene) nodes is always abnormal; these nodes drain the lung and retroperitoneum and can reflect lymphoma, other cancers, or infection. Small submandibular and up-to-2-cm inguinal nodes can be normal.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Enlargement of supraclavicular and scalene nodes is always abnormal."

items.question: An enlarged left supraclavicular node infiltrated with metastatic cancer classically points to a primary in which location?
items.explanation: Virchow's node is an enlarged left supraclavicular node infiltrated with metastatic cancer from a gastrointestinal primary. Supraclavicular metastases can also come from lung, breast, testis, or ovarian cancers.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Virchow's node is an enlarged left supraclavicular node infiltrated with metastatic cancer from a gastrointestinal primary."

items.question: On examination, a group of nodes is large, discrete, symmetric, rubbery, firm, mobile, and nontender. Which diagnosis does this texture most suggest?
items.explanation: Nodes involved by lymphoma tend to be large, discrete, symmetric, rubbery, firm, mobile, and nontender. Metastatic nodes are typically hard and fixed; acute inflammatory nodes are tender from capsular stretch.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Nodes involved by lymphoma tend to be large, discrete, symmetric, rubbery, firm, mobile, and nontender."

items.question: A hard, nontender, nonmovable node fixed to surrounding tissues is most consistent with which process?
items.explanation: Nodes containing metastatic cancer are often hard, nontender, and nonmovable because of fixation to surrounding tissues, in contrast to the rubbery mobile nodes of lymphoma.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Nodes containing metastatic cancer are often hard, nontender, and nonmovable because of fixation to surrounding tissues."

items.question: A 65-year-old smoker has a solitary, hard, nontender cervical node and no mucosal lesion on careful ENT examination. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Excisional biopsy of the largest node
items.explanation: When malignancy is suspected and no mucosal primary is found on ENT exam, excisional biopsy of the largest node is indicated. FNA should not be the first procedure (insufficient tissue, delays diagnosis), and empiric antibiotics/steroids are inappropriate.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "If no mucosal lesion is detected, an excisional biopsy of the largest node should be performed. Fine-needle aspiration should not be performed as the first diagnostic procedure."

items.question: Fine-needle aspiration is appropriately reserved for which of the following?
options: Initial diagnosis of an enlarging cervical node of unknown cause
Thyroid nodules and confirmation of relapse when the primary diagnosis is known
items.explanation: FNA should be reserved for thyroid nodules and for confirmation of relapse in patients whose primary diagnosis is already known; it should not be the first diagnostic procedure for undiagnosed lymphadenopathy.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Fine-needle aspiration should be reserved for thyroid nodules and for confirmation of relapse in patients whose primary diagnosis is known."

items.question: Why are glucocorticoids generally contraindicated for undiagnosed lymphadenopathy?
options: Their lympholytic effect obscures diagnoses such as lymphoma, leukemia, and Castleman's disease
They prevent any future biopsy from being interpretable for 6 months
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids should not be used because their lympholytic effect obscures diagnoses (lymphoma, leukemia, Castleman's disease) and they delay healing or activate underlying infections. The exception is airway-threatening Waldeyer's ring obstruction in mononucleosis.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Glucocorticoids should not be used to treat lymphadenopathy because their lympholytic effect obscures some diagnoses (lymphoma, leukemia, Castleman's disease) and they contribute to delayed healing or activation of underlying infections."

items.question: A 30-year-old has a 1-cm mobile cervical node, a benign history and examination, and no systemic symptoms. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance with reevaluation at a 2- to 4-week interval and return if nodes enlarge
CT of the neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis
items.explanation: When the history and examination point to a benign cause, careful follow-up at a 2- to 4-week interval is appropriate, with instructions to return if the nodes enlarge. Most such patients need neither biopsy nor laboratory studies.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "If the patient's history and physical findings point to a benign cause for lymphadenopathy, careful follow-up at a 2- to 4-week interval can be employed."

items.question: How is generalized lymphadenopathy defined?
options: Involvement of three or more noncontiguous lymph node areas
items.explanation: Generalized adenopathy is defined as involvement of three or more noncontiguous lymph node areas; localized (regional) adenopathy involves a single anatomic area.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Generalized adenopathy has been defined as involvement of three or more noncontiguous lymph node areas."

items.question: Which medication is a recognized cause of drug-hypersensitivity lymphadenopathy that should be sought in the history?
items.explanation: Diphenylhydantoin (phenytoin) is specifically cited; other culprits include hydralazine, allopurinol, primidone, gold, and carbamazepine. Recognizing a drug cause avoids unnecessary biopsy.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "The patient's age, sex, occupation, exposure to pets, sexual behavior, and use of drugs such as diphenylhydantoin are other important historic points."

items.question: Enlarged intraabdominal or retroperitoneal lymph nodes are best characterized as:
items.explanation: Enlarged intraabdominal or retroperitoneal nodes are usually malignant — most often lymphoma, or germ cell tumors in young men — although tuberculosis can present as mesenteric lymphadenitis.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Enlarged intraabdominal or retroperitoneal nodes are usually malignant."

items.question: Howell-Jolly bodies and Heinz bodies are removed from circulating red cells by which splenic process?
items.explanation: Pitting is the process by which red-cell inclusions (parasites, Howell-Jolly bodies of nuclear residua, Heinz bodies of denatured hemoglobin) are pinched off as cells squeeze through the cord slits. Culling is the destruction of whole senescent/damaged cells.
items.reference: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN: "Red cell-inclusion bodies such as parasites...nuclear residua (Howell-Jolly bodies...), or denatured hemoglobin (Heinz bodies) are pinched off in the process of passing through the slits, a process called pitting."

items.question: Approximately what fraction of total body platelets is normally held in the spleen?
items.explanation: The normal human spleen contains approximately one-third of the total body platelets along with marginated neutrophils. Enlargement increases this pooling, contributing to hypersplenic thrombocytopenia.
items.reference: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN: "The normal human spleen contains approximately one-third of the total body platelets and a significant number of marginated neutrophils."

items.question: What is the current procedure of choice for routine assessment of spleen size?
items.explanation: Ultrasonography is the procedure of choice because it is sensitive, specific, safe, noninvasive, quick, mobile (bedside-capable), and less costly. Normal spleen has a maximum cephalocaudad diameter of 13 cm.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (Splenomegaly): "The latter technique is the current procedure of choice for routine assessment of spleen size...because it has high sensitivity and specificity and is safe, noninvasive, quick, mobile, and less costly."

items.question: A patient has massive splenomegaly with the spleen palpable >8 cm below the left costal margin. Which group of diagnoses is most likely?
options: Acute viral infection or reactive hyperplasia
Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, CLL, hairy cell leukemia, CML, myelofibrosis, or polycythemia vera
items.explanation: When the spleen is massively enlarged (palpable >8 cm below the costal margin or ≥1000 g), the differential narrows to non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, CLL, hairy cell leukemia, CML, myelofibrosis with myeloid metaplasia, or polycythemia vera.
items.reference: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: "The vast majority of such patients will have non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, hairy cell leukemia, chronic myeloid leukemia, myelofibrosis with myeloid metaplasia, or polycythemia vera."

items.question: Which combination best characterizes hypersplenism?
options: Splenomegaly, cytopenia(s), hypoplastic marrow, and no response to splenectomy
Splenomegaly, cytopenia(s), normal or hyperplastic marrow, and a response to splenectomy
Normal spleen size with isolated thrombocytosis
Splenomegaly with pancytosis and marrow fibrosis
items.explanation: Hypersplenism is characterized by splenomegaly, one or more cytopenias, a normal or hyperplastic bone marrow, and a response to splenectomy (though correction of granulocytopenia is sometimes not sustained).
items.reference: LABORATORY ASSESSMENT: "This condition is characterized by splenomegaly, cytopenia(s), normal or hyperplastic bone marrow, and a response to splenectomy."

items.question: Which organism poses the greatest risk of overwhelming postsplenectomy sepsis, especially in patients under 20?
items.explanation: Asplenic patients are most susceptible to encapsulated organisms, and patients under 20 are particularly prone to overwhelming sepsis with Streptococcus pneumoniae; pneumococcal sepsis carries a 50–80% case-fatality rate in this group.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "Patients aged <20 years are particularly susceptible to overwhelming sepsis with S. pneumoniae, and the overall actuarial risk of sepsis in patients who have had their spleen[s] removed is about 7% in 10 years."

items.question: Regarding pneumococcal vaccination around elective splenectomy, which statement is correct?
options: It should be given on the day of surgery
It should be administered 2 weeks before elective splenectomy
It is contraindicated in asplenic patients
It replaces the need for any meningococcal vaccine
items.explanation: Pneumococcal vaccine should be administered to all patients 2 weeks before elective splenectomy; meningococcal vaccine should also be given, and Hib vaccine may be given. Vaccinating ahead of surgery optimizes the antibody response.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "Pneumococcal vaccine should be administered to all patients 2 weeks before elective splenectomy."

items.question: Beyond encapsulated bacteria, splenectomized patients have increased susceptibility to which parasitic disease?
items.explanation: Splenectomized patients are more susceptible to babesiosis and should avoid areas where Babesia is endemic (e.g., Cape Cod, Massachusetts). No increased risk of viral infection has been noted after splenectomy.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "In addition to an increased susceptibility to bacterial infections, splenectomized patients are also more susceptible to the parasitic disease babesiosis."

items.statement: In primary care practice, more than two-thirds of patients with lymphadenopathy have nonspecific causes or upper respiratory illnesses, and fewer than 1% have a malignancy.
items.explanation: This is the quoted epidemiology that justifies a conservative, observation-first approach in primary care.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (Lymphadenopathy): "In primary care practice, more than two-thirds of patients with lymphadenopathy have nonspecific causes or upper respiratory illnesses (viral or bacterial) and <1% have a malignancy."

items.statement: Enlargement of supraclavicular and scalene nodes can usually be regarded as a normal finding.
items.explanation: False — enlargement of supraclavicular and scalene nodes is always abnormal and warrants investigation, as they drain the lung and retroperitoneum.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Enlargement of supraclavicular and scalene nodes is always abnormal."

items.statement: Fine-needle aspiration is the preferred first diagnostic procedure for an undiagnosed enlarging lymph node.
items.explanation: False — FNA should not be the first procedure because most diagnoses need more tissue than it provides and it often delays diagnosis; excisional biopsy of the largest node is preferred.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Fine-needle aspiration should not be performed as the first diagnostic procedure."

items.statement: Glucocorticoids are a recommended empiric treatment to shrink undiagnosed enlarged lymph nodes.
items.explanation: False — glucocorticoids obscure diagnoses such as lymphoma, leukemia, and Castleman's disease and can activate infection; the only exception is airway-threatening Waldeyer's ring obstruction in mononucleosis.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Glucocorticoids should not be used to treat lymphadenopathy because their lympholytic effect obscures some diagnoses (lymphoma, leukemia, Castleman's disease)..."

items.statement: In head and neck cancer, an ultrasonographic long-to-short axis (L/S) ratio of <2.0 has a sensitivity and specificity of about 95% for distinguishing benign from malignant cervical nodes.
items.explanation: True — the L/S ratio <2.0 carries 95% sensitivity and specificity and outperforms palpation or either axis alone.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "An L/S ratio of <2.0 has a sensitivity and a specificity of 95% for distinguishing benign and malignant nodes in patients with head and neck cancer."

items.statement: The normal human spleen stores red blood cells and contracts under β-adrenergic stimulation to autotransfuse them during stress.
items.explanation: False — although this occurs in some animals, the normal human spleen does NOT sequester or store red cells and does not contract to sympathetic stimuli; it does normally hold about one-third of body platelets.
items.reference: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN: "However, the normal human spleen does not sequester or store red blood cells and does not contract in response to sympathetic stimuli."

items.statement: A palpable spleen always indicates underlying disease.
items.explanation: False — a palpable spleen was found in 3% of healthy young men and persisted in many without increased disease, and tropical splenomegaly can reach 60% incidence; a palpable spleen does not always equate with disease.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (Splenomegaly): "Thus, the presence of a palpable spleen does not always equate with presence of disease."

items.statement: Accessory spleens may develop in around 20% of persons when the embryologic splenic hillocks fail to unify.
items.explanation: True — failure of the hillocks to unify into a single tissue mass leads to accessory spleens in roughly 20% of people; nuclear scans can demonstrate accessory splenic tissue.
items.reference: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN: "When the hillocks fail to unify into a single tissue mass, accessory spleens may develop in around 20% of persons."

items.statement: Howell-Jolly bodies and target/abnormal red-cell forms appearing in a patient whose spleen has NOT been removed should raise suspicion of splenic infiltration interfering with culling and pitting.
items.explanation: True — these red-cell changes are expected after splenectomy; their appearance in someone with an intact spleen suggests tumor infiltration impairing the spleen's normal culling and pitting function.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "When such erythrocyte abnormalities appear in a patient whose spleen has not been removed, one should suspect splenic infiltration by tumor that has interfered with its normal culling and pitting function."

items.statement: Marrow failure, in which the enlarged spleen is the only source of hematopoietic tissue, is essentially a contraindication to splenectomy.
items.explanation: True — perhaps the only contraindication to splenectomy is marrow failure where the spleen is the sole site of (extramedullary) hematopoiesis.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "Perhaps the only contraindication to splenectomy is the presence of marrow failure, in which the enlarged spleen is the only source of hematopoietic tissue."

items.statement: Routine lifelong oral penicillin chemoprophylaxis is recommended for all splenectomized adults.
items.explanation: False — routine chemoprophylaxis with oral penicillin can lead to drug-resistant strains and is not recommended; instead, asplenic patients should treat any unexplained fever as an emergency and be vaccinated.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "Routine chemoprophylaxis with oral penicillin can result in the emergence of drug-resistant strains and is not recommended."

items.assertion: A supraclavicular node should be investigated even when it is small and nontender.
items.reason: Supraclavicular and scalene nodes drain the lung and retroperitoneal space, so their enlargement can reflect malignancy or serious infection arising in those areas.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion: because of their drainage territory, supraclavicular node enlargement is always abnormal and warrants workup regardless of size.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: "Because these nodes drain regions of the lung and retroperitoneal space, they can reflect lymphomas, other cancers, or infectious processes arising in these areas."

items.assertion: Fine-needle aspiration is the preferred first procedure to diagnose an undiagnosed enlarging node.
items.reason: Most diagnoses require more tissue than aspiration can provide, and FNA often delays definitive diagnosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false (FNA is NOT the first procedure; excisional biopsy is), while the reason is true and is precisely why FNA is not first-line. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Fine-needle aspiration should not be performed as the first diagnostic procedure. Most diagnoses require more tissue than such aspiration can provide, and it often delays a definitive diagnosis."

items.assertion: Empiric glucocorticoids should be avoided in undiagnosed lymphadenopathy.
items.reason: Their lympholytic effect can obscure diagnoses such as lymphoma, leukemia, and Castleman's disease.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because steroids lyse lymphoid tissue and mask these diagnoses (and can activate infection), they should not be used empirically.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Glucocorticoids should not be used to treat lymphadenopathy because their lympholytic effect obscures some diagnoses (lymphoma, leukemia, Castleman's disease)..."

items.assertion: In a normal person, sympathetic stress causes the human spleen to contract and autotransfuse stored red cells.
items.reason: The normal human spleen contains approximately one-third of the total body platelets.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — the normal human spleen does not store red cells or contract to sympathetic stimuli (unlike some animals). The reason is true (it holds ~1/3 of platelets) but does not support the false assertion. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN: "However, the normal human spleen does not sequester or store red blood cells and does not contract in response to sympathetic stimuli."

items.assertion: A palpable spleen in an asymptomatic young adult mandates an extensive malignancy workup.
items.reason: A palpable spleen is found in about 3% of healthy young men and often persists without any increase in disease prevalence.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — a palpable spleen does not always equate with disease and does not by itself mandate extensive workup. The reason is true and actually undercuts the assertion. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (Splenomegaly): "a palpable spleen was found in 3% of 2200 asymptomatic, male, freshman college students. Follow-up at 3 years revealed that 30% of those students still had a palpable spleen without any increase in disease prevalence."

items.assertion: Noninvasive imaging of the spleen alone is not a sufficiently reliable basis for staging decisions in Hodgkin's lymphoma.
items.reason: One-third of normal-sized spleens will be involved with Hodgkin's lymphoma and one-third of enlarged spleens will be tumor-free.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because spleen size correlates poorly with involvement, imaging cannot reliably stage the spleen in Hodgkin's lymphoma.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "Noninvasive staging of the spleen in Hodgkin's lymphoma is not a sufficiently reliable basis for treatment decisions because one-third of normal-sized spleens will be involved with Hodgkin's lymphoma and one-third of enlarged spleens will be tumor-free."

items.assertion: Asplenic patients are especially susceptible to infection with encapsulated organisms.
items.reason: Asplenia impairs the removal of opsonized bacteria from the bloodstream and the production of antibodies to T cell-independent antigens such as polysaccharide capsules.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: loss of splenic clearance of opsonized bacteria and impaired anti-polysaccharide antibody responses underlie the susceptibility to encapsulated organisms.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "The susceptibility to bacterial infections relates to the inability to remove opsonized bacteria from the bloodstream and a defect in making antibodies to T cell-independent antigens such as the polysaccharide components of bacterial capsules."

items.assertion: A palpable spleen in a patient with sickle cell disease after age 5 suggests a coexisting hemoglobinopathy.
items.reason: Patients with sickle cell disease often undergo autosplenectomy from repeated infarcts during childhood sickle cell crises.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because repeated infarcts usually destroy the spleen (autosplenectomy) by childhood, a persistently palpable spleen implies a modifying condition such as coexisting thalassemia or hemoglobin C.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "Patients with sickle cell disease often suffer from autosplenectomy as a result of splenic destruction by the numerous infarcts associated with sickle cell crises during childhood."

items.assertion: A normal-appearing spleen on CT or ultrasonography reliably excludes splenic involvement by Hodgkin's lymphoma.
items.reason: None of the imaging techniques is very reliable in the detection of patchy infiltration such as Hodgkin's lymphoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — imaging cannot reliably exclude splenic Hodgkin's involvement. The reason is true and explains why the assertion fails (patchy infiltration is poorly detected). False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (Splenomegaly): "None of these techniques is very reliable in the detection of patchy infiltration (e.g., Hodgkin's lymphoma)."

items.assertion: Splenectomy is sometimes performed for symptom control in chronic myeloid leukemia even though it does not alter the natural history of the disease.
items.reason: Removal of a massive spleen makes patients significantly more comfortable and simplifies management by reducing transfusion requirements.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: splenectomy in CML is palliative (comfort, fewer transfusions) rather than disease-modifying.
items.reference: SPLENECTOMY: "Although splenectomy in chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) does not affect the natural history of disease, removal of the massive spleen usually makes patients significantly more comfortable and simplifies their management by significantly reducing transfusion requirements."

items.subtopic: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen | LYMPHADENOPATHY | Lymphadenopathy | CLINICAL ASSESSMENT
items.title: Normal vs. Abnormal Lymph Node Size
items.content: Determine whether lymphadenopathy requires further investigation based on node size and patient demographics.
keyPoints: Soft, flat submandibular nodes <1 cm are normal in children and young adults.
Inguinal nodes up to 2 cm can be normal in healthy adults.
Nodes larger than these measurements or with concerning features may indicate abnormality requiring further evaluation.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen | LYMPHADENOPATHY | Lymphadenopathy | CLINICAL ASSESSMENT

items.subtopic: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen | LYMPHADENOPATHY | Lymphadenopathy | CLINICAL ASSESSMENT
items.title: Etiology of Lymphadenopathy in Primary Care
items.content: Understand the common causes of lymphadenopathy to guide diagnostic testing.
keyPoints: More than two-thirds of lymphadenopathy cases are due to nonspecific causes or upper respiratory illnesses (viral or bacterial).
Less than 1% of patients with lymphadenopathy have malignancy.
84% of referred patients for lymphadenopathy evaluation receive a benign diagnosis.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen | LYMPHADENOPATHY | Lymphadenopathy | CLINICAL ASSESSMENT

items.subtopic: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen | LYMPHADENOPATHY | Lymphadenopathy | CLINICAL ASSESSMENT
items.title: Age-Related Considerations in Lymphadenopathy
items.content: Consider age when evaluating lymphadenopathy to differentiate between benign and malignant causes.
keyPoints: Children and young adults often have benign causes like viral or bacterial infections, infectious mononucleosis, toxoplasmosis, or tuberculosis.
After age 50, the likelihood of malignancy increases while benign disorders decrease.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen | LYMPHADENOPATHY | Lymphadenopathy | CLINICAL ASSESSMENT

items.title: Significance of Supraclavicular Lymphadenopathy
items.content: Supraclavicular lymph node enlargement is always abnormal and may indicate serious underlying conditions such as malignancies or infections.
keyPoints: Enlargement of supraclavicular nodes can reflect lymphomas, other cancers, or infectious processes from the lung and retroperitoneal space.
Virchow's node: Enlarged left supraclavicular node with metastatic cancer from a gastrointestinal primary.
Metastases to supraclavicular nodes also occur from lung, breast, testis, or ovarian cancers.
Nonneoplastic causes include tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, and toxoplasmosis.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Thresholds for Lymph Node Biopsy
items.content: Lymph node size is a critical parameter in determining the need for biopsy to diagnose malignant or granulomatous disease.
keyPoints: Nodes <1.0 cm² are usually benign and reactive, often not requiring immediate intervention unless systemic illness symptoms are present.
A lymph node diameter >2 cm in younger patients (9–25 years) is a discriminant for predicting malignant or granulomatous disease.
Lymph node size of 2.25 cm² is the best limit to distinguish between malignant/granulomatous and other causes of lymphadenopathy.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of Lymph Node Texture in Diagnosis
items.content: The texture of lymph nodes provides valuable diagnostic clues, differentiating between benign/reactive and malignant conditions.
keyPoints: Tenderness indicates rapid enlargement due to an inflammatory process.
Malignant diseases like acute leukemia can cause rapid enlargement with pain.
Lymphoma-involved nodes are large, discrete, symmetric, rubbery, firm, mobile, and nontender.
Nodes with metastatic cancer are hard, nontender, and nonmovable due to fixation.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 1)

items.title: When to Consider Lymph Node Biopsy
items.content: Lymph node biopsy is crucial for diagnosing malignancies, especially in cases with specific clinical features such as solitary hard cervical nodes in older patients or supraclavicular adenopathy. Consultation with specialists may be necessary when the primary physician is uncertain.
keyPoints: Prompt biopsy indicated if history and physical findings suggest malignancy (e.g., solitary, hard, nontender cervical node in an older patient).
Fine-needle aspiration should not be first-line; excisional biopsy preferred for definitive diagnosis.
<5% of lymphadenopathy patients require biopsy in primary care, higher in referral practices.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 2) | LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 1)

items.title: Imaging Techniques for Differentiating Lymph Nodes
items.content: CT and MRI are comparably accurate in diagnosing metastases to cervical lymph nodes, with ultrasonography providing high specificity and sensitivity using the L/S ratio.
keyPoints: CT and MRI accuracy: 65–90% for diagnosing metastases to cervical lymph nodes.
Ultrasonography L/S ratio <2.0 has 95% sensitivity and specificity for distinguishing benign from malignant nodes in head and neck cancer patients.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 2) | LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 1)

items.title: Predictive Models for Lymph Node Biopsy
items.content: Retrospective studies have identified variables predicting the need for biopsy in lymphadenopathy patients, with specific models showing high predictive values.
keyPoints: Study on young patients: Lymph node size >2 cm and abnormal chest x-ray had positive predictive value; recent ENT symptoms had negative predictive value.
Hematology unit study identified age >40 years, supraclavicular location, node size >2.25 cm², hard texture, and lack of pain as predictors for biopsy with 91% correct classification.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 2) | LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN
items.title: When is a biopsy unnecessary in lymphadenopathy?
items.content: In most cases of lymphadenopathy, a biopsy is not required. If the clinical evaluation suggests a benign cause, follow-up at 2-4 weeks can be sufficient unless there's an increase in node size.
keyPoints: Most patients with lymphadenopathy do not need a biopsy.
Follow-up at 2- to 4-week intervals is often adequate for benign cases.
Biopsy or further investigation should be considered if nodes enlarge.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN

items.subtopic: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN
items.title: Guidelines for using glucocorticoids in lymphadenopathy
items.content: Glucocorticoids should generally be avoided in treating lymphadenopathy due to their potential to obscure certain diagnoses and delay healing. An exception is life-threatening pharyngeal obstruction from enlarged lymphoid tissue in infectious mononucleosis.
keyPoints: Avoid glucocorticoids for lymphadenopathy unless specific conditions like pharyngeal obstruction are present.
Glucocorticoids can obscure diagnoses such as lymphoma, leukemia, and Castleman's disease.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN

items.subtopic: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN
items.title: Physiological roles of the spleen in erythrocyte maintenance
items.content: The spleen maintains quality control over erythrocytes by removing senescent and defective red blood cells through its unique parenchymal organization. It also plays a role in antibody synthesis, removal of coated bacteria, and extramedullary hematopoiesis.
keyPoints: Spleen removes old and damaged erythrocytes via the reticuloendothelial system.
Blood flow through the spleen is about 150 mL/min.
The spleen can perform extramedullary hematopoiesis when necessary.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN

items.title: Understanding Splenic Pain and Its Causes
items.content: Splenic pain can result from acute swelling, infarction, or inflammation of the capsule. While historically considered clinically silent, splenic infarction may present with severe left upper quadrant (LUQ) and pleuritic chest pain.
keyPoints: Pain due to acute swelling, infarction, or inflammation
Historical belief: Splenic infarction was often clinically silent
Soma Weiss documented splenic infarction with severe LUQ and pleuritic chest pain in 1942
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Interpreting a Palpable Spleen
items.content: A palpable spleen suggests enlargement but does not always indicate disease. It may be found in asymptomatic individuals and can persist without increased disease prevalence over time.
keyPoints: Normal spleen weight: <250 g, maximum cephalocaudad diameter by ultrasonography: 13 cm
3% of 2200 asymptomatic male freshmen had a palpable spleen; 30% still had it after 3 years without increased disease prevalence
Ten-year follow-up showed no evidence for lymphoid malignancies
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Techniques for Physical Examination of the Spleen
items.content: Physical examination techniques include palpation and percussion. Bimanual palpation is reliable, while Nixon's, Castell's, and Barkun's methods are used for percussion to assess splenic dullness.
keyPoints: Bimanual palpation: Patient supine with flexed knees; spleen tip recorded in cm below left costal margin
Nixon's method: Dullness >8 cm above the costal margin indicates splenic enlargement
Castell's method: Dull note on full inspiration suggests splenomegaly
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: When to Use Imaging for Splenomegaly Assessment
items.content: Physical examination techniques like palpation and percussion have limited sensitivity, especially in obese patients or those who have recently eaten. Ultrasonography is recommended as the first-line imaging technique due to its high sensitivity, specificity, safety, and cost-effectiveness.
keyPoints: Sensitivity of palpation: 56–71%
Sensitivity of percussion: 59–82%
Ultrasonography preferred for routine assessment
Normal spleen size: maximum cephalocaudad diameter of 13 cm
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis in Massive Splenomegaly
items.content: Massive splenomegaly, defined as a spleen palpable >8 cm below the left costal margin or drained weight ≥1000 g, is most commonly associated with hematologic malignancies. The primary differential diagnoses include non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, hairy cell leukemia, chronic myeloid leukemia, myelofibrosis with myeloid metaplasia, and polycythemia vera.
keyPoints: Massive splenomegaly: palpable >8 cm below left costal margin
Drained weight ≥1000 g indicates massive enlargement
Common causes: non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, hairy cell leukemia, chronic myeloid leukemia, myelofibrosis with myeloid metaplasia, polycythemia vera
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Evaluating Cytopenias in Splenomegaly: Indicators of Hypersplenism
items.content: In patients with splenomegaly, the presence of cytopenia(s) suggests hypersplenism. This condition is characterized by splenomegaly, cytopenia(s), normal or hyperplastic bone marrow, and a response to splenectomy. Cytopenias result from increased destruction due to reduced blood flow through congested spleens or immune-mediated mechanisms.
keyPoints: Cytopenias in splenomegaly suggest hypersplenism.
Hypersplenism is characterized by splenomegaly, cytopenia(s), normal or hyperplastic bone marrow, and response to splenectomy.
Cytopenias result from increased destruction due to reduced blood flow or immune-mediated mechanisms.
Peripheral blood smear usually shows normal morphology of cells; red cells may be spherocytic.
Increased marrow production should reflect as an increased reticulocyte production index, though it might be less than expected due to sequestration.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.title: Interpreting Blood Counts in Splenomegaly: Systemic Illness Indicators
items.content: The major laboratory abnormalities accompanying splenomegaly are determined by the underlying systemic illness. Erythrocyte, granulocyte, and platelet counts can vary based on conditions like thalassemia, SLE, polycythemia vera, infections, or myeloproliferative disorders.
keyPoints: Erythrocyte counts in splenomegaly may be normal, decreased (thalassemia major syndromes, SLE, cirrhosis with portal hypertension), or increased (polycythemia vera).
Granulocyte counts can be normal, decreased (Felty's syndrome, congestive splenomegaly, leukemias), or increased (infections/inflammatory disease, myeloproliferative disorders).
Platelet count may be normal, decreased (congestive splenomegaly, Gaucher's disease, immune thrombocytopenia), or increased (myeloproliferative disorders like polycythemia vera).
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.title: Indications for Splenectomy
items.content: Splenectomy is performed for symptom control in massive splenomegaly, disease control after traumatic splenic rupture, and correction of cytopenias due to hypersplenism or immune-mediated destruction. It's necessary for staging Hodgkin's lymphoma in clinical stage I or II when radiation therapy alone is considered.
keyPoints: Splenectomy is rarely diagnostic unless other tests suggest underlying disease.
Used for symptom control in massive splenomegaly and traumatic rupture.
Corrects cytopenias in hypersplenism or immune-mediated destruction.
Necessary for Hodgkin's lymphoma staging in clinical stage I or II when radiation therapy alone is considered.
Staging laparotomy with splenectomy is unnecessary due to systemic therapy advancements.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 1)

items.title: Effects and Risks of Splenectomy
items.content: Splenectomy can lead to significant comfort improvement in CML by reducing transfusion needs, though it doesn't alter disease progression. It's effective for certain chronic B-cell leukemias and may cause systemic tumor regression. Laparoscopic splenectomy is associated with faster recovery but higher risk of postoperative portal venous thrombosis.
keyPoints: Splenectomy does not affect CML natural history but reduces transfusion needs.
Effective for hairy cell leukemia, prolymphocytic leukemia, and rare lymphomas.
Laparoscopic splenectomy offers faster recovery with higher risk of portal venous thrombosis.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 1)

items.title: Post-Splenectomy Hematologic Changes
items.content: After splenectomy, leukocytosis and thrombocytosis may occur but normalize within weeks. Long-term effects include anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, Howell-Jolly bodies, Heinz bodies, basophilic stippling, and occasional nucleated erythrocytes.
keyPoints: Leukocytosis (up to 25,000/μL) and thrombocytosis (up to $1 	imes 10^{6}/μ L$) may occur post-splenectomy but normalize within 2-3 weeks.
Long-term effects include anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, Howell-Jolly bodies, Heinz bodies, basophilic stippling, and occasional nucleated erythrocytes.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 1)

items.title: Increased Risk of Bacterial Infections Post-Splenectomy
items.content: Splenectomy significantly increases susceptibility to bacterial infections, especially from encapsulated organisms like Streptococcus pneumoniae. This risk is highest within the first three years post-surgery and remains elevated over time.
keyPoints: Increased susceptibility to bacterial infections such as Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and some gram-negative enteric organisms.
Actuarial risk of sepsis in splenectomized patients is about 7% over 10 years.
Case-fatality rate for pneumococcal sepsis in these patients is 50–80%.
25% of splenectomized patients will develop a serious infection at some point in their life, with the highest frequency within the first 3 years post-splenectomy.
15% of infections are polymicrobial; common sites include lung, skin, and blood.
Pneumococcal vaccine should be administered 2 weeks before elective splenectomy, with a repeat vaccination recommended at 5 years post-splenectomy.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 2)

items.title: Management and Prophylaxis in Splenectomized Patients
items.content: Splenectomized patients should receive specific vaccinations and be educated on the risks of infections. Routine chemoprophylaxis is not recommended due to potential drug resistance.
keyPoints: Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) involves T cells in response, offering more effective protection.
Neisseria meningitidis vaccine should be administered pre-splenectomy.
H. influenzae type B vaccine may be given post-splenectomy despite lack of efficacy data for older children/adults.
Routine chemoprophylaxis with oral penicillin is not recommended due to risk of drug-resistant strains.
Patients should consider any unexplained fever as a medical emergency and seek prompt attention.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 2)

items.question: What is the significance of generalized lymphadenopathy in adults?
options: It usually indicates a benign disorder.
It often suggests malignant disorders like leukemia or lymphoma.
It implies involvement of three or more noncontiguous lymph node areas.
Both B and C are correct.
items.explanation: Generalized lymphadenopathy is defined as involvement of three or more noncontiguous lymph node areas, often associated with both benign and malignant disorders.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a viral cause of lymphadenopathy mentioned in the passage?
items.explanation: Staphylococci is a bacterial cause, not viral.

items.question: What is the most frequent site of regional adenopathy?
items.explanation: The neck is the most frequent site for regional adenopathy.

items.question: Which lymph node enlargement is always considered abnormal?
items.explanation: Enlargement of supraclavicular and scalene nodes is always abnormal.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 1)

items.question: Which condition can cause unilateral paratracheal lymph node involvement that mimics lymphoma?
items.explanation: Histoplasmosis can cause unilateral paratracheal lymph node involvement that mimics lymphoma.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging technique has greater specificity and sensitivity than palpation or measurement of either the long or short axis alone for distinguishing benign from malignant cervical nodes in patients with head and neck cancer?
items.explanation: An L/S ratio of <2.0 using ultrasonography has greater specificity and sensitivity than palpation or measurement of either the long or short axis alone.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 2) | LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 1)

items.question: Which laboratory study is most commonly performed in patients with lymphadenopathy according to a family practice clinic evaluation?
items.explanation: The most common laboratory study performed was a complete blood count (CBC) at 33%.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 2) | LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 1)

items.question: In which scenario should prompt lymph node biopsy be considered?
options: A solitary, hard, nontender cervical node in an older patient who is a chronic user of tobacco
Generalized adenopathy that is soft and tender
items.explanation: Prompt biopsy should occur if the patient's history and physical findings suggest a malignancy, such as a solitary, hard, nontender cervical node in an older patient who is a chronic user of tobacco.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 2) | LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 1)

items.question: What does the presence of a pulmonary infiltrate or mediastinal lymphadenopathy on chest x-ray suggest?
options: Tuberculosis, histoplasmosis, sarcoidosis, lymphoma, primary lung cancer, or metastatic cancer
items.explanation: The presence of a pulmonary infiltrate or mediastinal lymphadenopathy would suggest tuberculosis, histoplasmosis, sarcoidosis, lymphoma, primary lung cancer, or metastatic cancer.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · 1. Infectious diseases (part 2) | LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason for avoiding glucocorticoids in treating lymphadenopathy?
options: They obscure some diagnoses such as lymphoma and leukemia.
They contribute to delayed healing or activation of underlying infections.
They are ineffective against bacterial infections.
Their lympholytic effect can mask the diagnosis.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids should be avoided because they obscure some diagnoses and contribute to delayed healing, not because they are ineffective against bacterial infections.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN

items.subtopic: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN
items.question: What is the primary reason for the spleen's involvement in the portal circulation?
options: To minimize damage to normal erythrocytes.
To increase the pressure in splenic arteries.
items.explanation: The spleen is involved in portal circulation because lower blood pressure allows less rapid flow and minimizes damage to normal erythrocytes.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN

items.subtopic: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN
items.question: What happens to red blood cells that are less deformable in the spleen?
options: They pass through the slits without issue.
They are destroyed and their components recycled.
They are stored for future use.
items.explanation: Old and damaged erythrocytes, which are less deformable, are retained in the cords where they are destroyed and their components recycled.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN

items.subtopic: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a normal physiologic role of the spleen?
options: Maintenance of quality control over erythrocytes.
Synthesis of antibodies in the white pulp.
Removal of antibody-coated bacteria from circulation.
Storage and contraction of red blood cells under sympathetic stimuli.
items.explanation: The normal human spleen does not store or contract red blood cells in response to sympathetic stimuli, unlike some animals.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (part 2) | STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION OF THE SPLEEN

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common symptom produced by diseases involving the spleen?
options: Pain in the left upper quadrant
Early satiety due to massive splenomegaly
items.explanation: Common symptoms include pain and a heavy sensation in the LUQ, as well as early satiety from massive splenomegaly.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following conditions is associated with splenic infarction and pain?
items.explanation: Splenic infarction and pain are commonly seen in children with sickle cell crises.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: What is the maximum cephalocaudad diameter of a normal spleen by ultrasonography?
items.explanation: The maximum cephalocaudad diameter of a normal spleen is 13 cm by ultrasonography.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: In which position does Nixon's method for percussion of splenic dullness place the patient?
items.explanation: Nixon's method places the patient on the right side to allow the spleen to lie above the colon and stomach.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging technique is currently the procedure of choice for routine assessment of spleen size?
items.explanation: Ultrasonography is the current procedure of choice due to its high sensitivity, specificity, safety, and cost-effectiveness.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition does NOT typically cause massive splenomegaly?
items.explanation: Hereditary spherocytosis typically causes splenomegaly due to reticuloendothelial hyperplasia, but not massive enlargement.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which technique is less reliable in detecting patchy infiltration such as Hodgkin's lymphoma?
items.explanation: None of these techniques are very reliable for detecting patchy infiltration like that seen in Hodgkin's lymphoma.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition is characterized by splenomegaly, cytopenia(s), normal or hyperplastic bone marrow, and a response to splenectomy?
items.explanation: Hypersplenism is characterized by these features.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.question: In hypersplenism, what is a common finding on the peripheral blood smear regarding red cells?
items.explanation: Red cells may be spherocytic due to loss of surface area during their longer transit through the enlarged spleen.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.question: What is a potential reason for reduced reticulocyte production index in hypersplenism?
options: Decreased marrow production of red cells
Increased sequestration of reticulocytes in the spleen
items.explanation: The value may be less than expected due to increased sequestration of reticulocytes in the spleen.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common indication for splenectomy?
options: Staging in all stages of Hodgkin's lymphoma
items.explanation: Splenectomy is necessary for staging only in clinical stage I or II Hodgkin's lymphoma when radiation therapy alone is contemplated.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 1)

items.question: What is a common long-term hematologic manifestation after splenectomy?
items.explanation: Long-term hematologic manifestations include anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, and the presence of Howell-Jolly bodies.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is associated with a higher risk when performing splenectomy laparoscopically?
items.explanation: Laparoscopic splenectomy is associated with a higher risk of postoperative portal venous system thrombosis.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 1)

items.question: What is the immediate hematologic change after splenectomy?
items.explanation: The immediate postsplenectomy period may show transient leukocytosis (up to 25,000/μL) and thrombocytosis.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following organisms is NOT associated with increased susceptibility to infection in splenectomized patients?
items.explanation: No increased risk of viral infection has been noted in patients who have no spleen.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended timing for administering pneumococcal vaccine to patients undergoing elective splenectomy?
items.explanation: Pneumococcal vaccine should be administered to all patients 2 weeks before elective splenectomy.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 2)

items.question: What is the actuarial risk of sepsis in patients who have had their spleens removed over a period of 10 years?
items.explanation: The overall actuarial risk of sepsis in patients who have had their spleeness removed is about 7% in 10 years.
items.reference: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen · SPLENECTOMY (part 2)

items.subtopic: Enlargement of Lymph Nodes and Spleen | LYMPHADENOPATHY | Lymphadenopathy | CLINICAL ASSESSMENT
items.statement: Palpable splenomegaly is more common in primary care settings than lymphadenopathy.
items.explanation: Lymphadenopathy is more common in primary care settings compared to palpable splenomegaly.
